Transcrição Completa: 5 1MM Trophy Talk - Daniel Fazio - ListKit
[00:00] "Oh, thanks, bro. What's up, guys? All right. Name is Daniel Fazio. I want to go through this as fast as possible. I assume you guys all want to get to the penthouse as expeditiously as possible. So, there's like 60 slides. I'll I'll ram through these as fast as possible."
[00:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "1MM Trophy Talk - Daniel Fazio - ListKit"
NÚMEROS: $1,001,168 em New Year's Eve, ~7pm, $997K às 7pm
VISUAL: Título da apresentação com achievement milestone
[00:28] "So, it was exactly 1,1,68 on New Year's Eve. It was probably like 7 p.m. It was like 997,000. I was like, 'Fuck, I got to sell someone something.' So, we were just we were just at the end of the night just trying to collect cash and we were able to pull it off."
[00:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What I'll Cover"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Agenda: My Funnel, Why Churn Doesn't Matter, Offers Working, AI for Sales Calls/VSSL/Ads
[00:58] "So, I'm going to cover my funnel, why churn doesn't matter, what to focus on instead, offers I see working and how to structure yours because I also run like 300k a month coaching business as well. So, I work with a lot of you like guys in everyone's area as well. Um, and then using AI to analyze sales calls, how to use it to write your VSSL and how to use it to write your ads."
[01:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Two Businesses"
NÚMEROS: $300K/month coaching
VISUAL: Clients (cash flow) vs ListKit (software for exit)
[01:32] "So, client that's for cash flow and list kit that's a software business. this for an exit."
[01:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Clients Funnel"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: YouTube → Twitter → LinkedIn → Optins → VSSL → Application → Sales Call
[01:48] "Um, this is what the funnel looks like for clients essentially. Just YouTube, Twitter, LinkedIn, optins. I'm going to be talking about optins a fair bit, just a little bit here. Hopefully Jeremy doesn't come up and fuck suplex me because I know he hates them. Um, VSSL, application, sales call, just literally same funnel as everyone else."
[02:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "ListKit Funnel"
NÚMEROS: 100% cold traffic, 0% warm
VISUAL: Meta Ads → Rest of funnel (all cold traffic)
[02:22] "List, same thing, but it's just metad. So, so zero cold traffic to the coaching. Zero. Absolutely zero. Send zero cold traffic to coaching ever. I never do that. But all of Liskit's traffic is cold. 100% of it, right? All of its new traffic."
[02:48] "So this picture isn't exactly the full story because it's deeper than that. Um, but in order for me to explain that, you got to understand a metric that is 30 times more important to turn."
[03:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Net Revenue Retention (NRR)"
NÚMEROS: 30x mais importante que churn
VISUAL: Conceito central da apresentação
[03:12] "So because we want to sell this kit, we talked to a lot of PE and VC companies trying to get valuations for it. And so what I'm going to talk about is my context as to what I learned from them. Like how do we sell this fuck for $100 million, right?"
[03:32] "And they're not concerned with churn per se. What they're actually concerned with is net revenue retention. NR. Has anyone ever heard of this metric at all? Ever? Just raise your hand. NR. So like fuck no one at all. It's really weird."
[03:55] "So So churn matters only to the extent that it impacts NR, right? So it's expressed as a percentage. It is quite literally the expression of the natural growth of your company."
[04:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NRR Explained"
NÚMEROS: 100% = upsells equal churn, 80% = dying, 110% = growing naturally
VISUAL: Fórmula e exemplos de diferentes percentuais
[04:22] "So for instance, 100% NR means that your upsells exactly equal your churn. 80% means your your churn outweighs your upsells by a degree of 20%. 110% NR means that your upsells would outweigh your churn by a degree of 10%. Right?"
[04:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "80% NRR = Death in 5 Months"
NÚMEROS: 5 meses para morrer com 80% NRR
VISUAL: Visualização de business atrophy
[04:55] "So So just to visualize this, a business with an NR below 100% will atrophy over time and die. Right? So if you have an NR of 80% and you fail to acquire any new customers, you will be dead in 5 months. Does that make sense to everyone?"
[05:18] "So you can see that if someone's going to acquire your company, it's super risky. You will just you will cease to exist in five months. It's super fast, right? Dog fuck business if you have an 80% NR, right?"
[05:38] "So it's the equivalent of being metabolically impaired, right? Low NR business means you require progressively more food to extract nutrients from, right? Start getting fat. It's literally what that is."
[05:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NRR Formula"
NÚMEROS: (Start + Upsells - Losses) / Start = NRR%
VISUAL: Fórmula matemática do NRR
[06:05] "So turn matters only to the extent that it impacts NR. So, it's what you start at plus what you upsell minus what you lose divided by what you started at, right? As a percent. Remember, this is super fuck important. All right?"
[06:28] "Does anyone here run a subscription service at all? Just just any kind of just retainer based business at all, right? You ever hear people say 3% churn or or this this really like you see those low ticket groups like $99 a month group. Anyone has anyone ever run a low ticket group? What's your turn on a low ticket group?"
[06:52] "Like fuck 20 25%. Yeah, always up there 100% of the time."
[07:02] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Churn Reality in SaaS"
NÚMEROS: 15-25% churn típico, 3% churn = impossível
VISUAL: Dados reais vs marketing claims
[07:08] "I've actually run six total software companies just just ram through a bunch of different ones. So even in SAS, I could see that it was very typical that you would see like anywhere from 15 to 25% churn."
[07:28] "So you you would talk to you you would talk to people and say like, oh, for a good valuation, you need 3% churn. I'm like, I'm not sure exactly how that's even conceivably possible. Like that that doesn't even make like how can you actually have 3% churn. It doesn't fuck compute, right?"
[07:52] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Churn Manipulation Tactics"
NÚMEROS: Não especificados
VISUAL: Lista de táticas: Pause plans ($1), Discounting (90% off), Yearly subscriptions, Data manipulation
[08:00] "So what actually is happening is nobody outside of enterprise ever has a churn of 3%. They just do little stupid data manipulation fuck like this like like pause plans like are you going to cancel? will go in a $1 plan. Now it's not churn, right?"
[08:22] "Aggressive discounting for people who are going to churn. Like let them take 90% off, right? Or will another one here substantially increasing ad spend to offers with a yearly subscription as to outweigh the monthly churn. Because if you sell it with a yearly subscription, you're automatically locking in 12 months of retention, right?"
[08:48] "You fuck roll all the cash flow into that. Manipulate the churn number. That's why churn isn't a good measure of the health of the business. It has to be NR because there's manipulation fuck like this. You see what I mean? It doesn't say anything."
[09:12] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Only Way to Make $3M-10M/Month"
NÚMEROS: $3M-10M/month range
VISUAL: Optimize NRR como estratégia principal
[09:18] "So therefore, the only way really to make a fuck ton of money, like 3 million to 10 million a month, is through optimizing NR. That's kind of what I learned talking to these like VC PE companies and fuck They really just don't give a fuck about churn at all. It's just what can you upsell in relation to what you're losing on churn, right?"
[09:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NRR Math Example"
NÚMEROS: 300 customers, $1K/month, 15% churn = need $45K/month upsells for 100% NRR
VISUAL: Cálculo prático de upsells necessários
[09:55] "So just to help you visualize that, if you have 300 customers at $1,000 a month and your monthly turn's 15%. and you want your NR to be 100%. You need to upsell $45,000 per month."
[10:18] "You see what I mean? So that would mean 45 people leave. If all of them were at 1,00,000 a month, you'd have to go upsell 45 of the people to 2,000 a month. That's a lot of fuck people to upsell, right?"
[10:38] "So you can see that this is a whole ass operation that goes on, right? It's it's not like people don't just upsell. It's a very intentional act to the point where you have to build a fuck whole sales team around it. Right?"
[10:58] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Real Funnel - Portfolio of Products"
NÚMEROS: Múltiplos produtos
VISUAL: Cold email setup, Email leads, Email verifier, Email sending, Data streams, Website visitors
[11:05] "So, I was getting that before the picture of the funnel where it said this is what the actual funnel looks like. It's a portfolio of different products. You just sell all different kinds of fuck right?"
[11:22] "Cold email setup offer, email leads, email verifier, email sending, data streams, website visitors."
[11:35] "It's also another reason why I use optin. Someone comes in for some offer, they don't book a call for it, they're not interested whatsoever. We're hammering them three emails a week. Over time, they become aware of all the offers."
[11:55] "They get retargeted with ads for all the all the different offers. Maybe they come in to a different offer, right? Or they get in a sales call for one, it actually makes sense for them to get another one and then they go to the CS team and the CS team can upsell them to another product later on."
[12:18] "You could cross-ell and bundle fuck together. You just sell a fuck ton of fuck"
[12:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Breakthrough"
NÚMEROS: Mid 2023-Early 2024: stuck at $300-400K/month, Understanding NRR → straight to $1M/month
VISUAL: Timeline de crescimento
[12:35] "And that like early 2024, from probably mid 2023 to early 2024, we were stuck at like 300 to 400k a month. just could not get past that. Right? When we understood this concept, just continuously make more fuck fuck straight to a million a month. Easy."
[13:00] "And it wasn't even hard. It was just this. You just launch more fuck for people to buy, right? So, you you just need so much stuff that it it it just doesn't make sense. It's it's like difficult for them to completely leave, right?"
[13:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "NRR Boosters - Dedicated Sales Team"
NÚMEROS: 7,000 customers, full-time reps prospecting active customers
VISUAL: Conceito de sales team focado em upsell
[13:28] "So, um, like I was saying earlier, you're trying to just ram the maximum amount of people into this funnel to the point where you just have like fuck thousands of them to where you hire full-time sales reps just to prospect against your active paying customers."
[13:52] "We literally call them NR boosters. It's a sales team who does outbound dialing and outbound email just to the people who are already customers. There's like 7,000 of them. You see what I mean? It's just just just selling against the people you already have."
[14:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Math at $1M/Month"
NÚMEROS: $1M/month, 15% churn = need $150K/month in upsells, For 110% NRR = $250K/month upsells
VISUAL: Cálculos de upsell necessários em escala
[14:25] "So, just to help you visualize it a bit, if you're at one one million a month on a recurring offer with a 15% monthly turn, which is normal for a lot of people in here, you need to make $150,000 per month exclusively upselling fuck to active people already, right?"
[14:52] "So if you want NR to be 110% like HubSpot, there was some there was some interview with Darm mesh and he he dropped something so nonchalantly and it's very interesting. He was talking about how the early days of HubSpot their NR was 80% and now it's like either 110% or 115%."
[15:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "HubSpot Case Study"
NÚMEROS: 80% NRR early days → 110-115% NRR now, 14 different subscriptions
VISUAL: HubSpot interface com múltiplos produtos
[15:25] "Does anyone here use HubSpot? You know how like you go into the you like you fuck around in the tabs of HubSpot, there's so much fuck to buy. There's like there's like 14 different subscriptions you can buy under a singular HubSpot account. That's why their NR is 110%. There's just hella fuck to buy. You see what I mean?"
[15:52] "So, it's no wonder that they can not acquire customers and still naturally grow because the NR is 110%. Right? So, it it's almost difficult to fuck this up if you have if you have enough products. You see what I mean?"
[16:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "December Results"
NÚMEROS: $1M revenue, $200K from upsells/cross-sells, $194K ad spend, 1.3x front-end ROAS
VISUAL: Breakdown de revenue sources
[16:25] "So, December, we hit our first one million month. About $200,000 of that was from upselles and cross sales to current customers. That's why we were able to hit a million a month with only 194k of ad spend. Right?"
[16:48] "So, someone might see, oh, you spent 194k in ads and made a million. You had a 5x rowass. No, it was actually like a 1.3x rowass on the front end."
[17:05] "But, but how does this compute? Like, how did that happen? It's because over the previous six months, we spent a total of 1 million on ads and then that just provided the quantity of customers required to upsell more fuck to. You see what I mean?"
[17:28] "You just do it for a long enough time frame. And if you go back and you look at old campaigns, if you use highros, you'll see that the rorowass just gradually goes up. So if I if I look at campaigns from like six months ago, the rorowass is actually like five or six, not 1.3, which is what we'll normally get in the front end. Does that make sense? Gradually goes up over time if you continue to sell them more fuck Right?"
[18:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Optins - When To Use"
NÚMEROS: $17.5K spend, 0.9x front-end ROAS, $240 cost per qualified call, 5 minutes average time to reach
VISUAL: Winning ads data com opt-ins
[18:08] "So yes, I'm sorry, Jeremy. I do use optins. Um, this is just an example of this was this was 17 and a halfk. It's these are winning ads, by the way. These were proven winning ads. 0.9x on the front end at $240 cost per qualified call."
[18:32] "And then these these are all with opt-ins. So the now the fuck rowass is so much higher. I think the the the reason why is just because we have setters, right? Uh when you should use you have you have setters who you should only use optins if you're going to have setters dial within five minutes. That's our average time to reach a person."
[18:58] "We get name, email, phone number on the opt-in. setters are reaching them within five minutes like pretty much. What's up?"
[19:08] "I advocate for optins when you're selling to the general public."
[19:15] "Dope. Good stuff. Um the problem the pro I think the the main problem that people have is if you try to use an optin, you'll put an optin and collect name, email, phone number, then you won't fuck do anything with with with the email or the phone number. It just it just makes absolutely zero sense."
[19:38] "If you're gonna have an optin, you have to get to the leads within five minutes. It there's literally no purpose of doing it at all unless you're doing something with the phone numbers, right?"
[19:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Email Strategy"
NÚMEROS: 1-3 broadcast emails/day, 2-4 total emails/day (com flows)

So, they'll get anywhere from like one to three broadcast emails per day in addition to what flow that they otherwise might be in from the original optin. So, they can get anywhere from like two to four emails a day. fuck ton of emails. Works best when you have multiple offers as I said."

[21:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Self-Descriptive Mechanisms"

[21:55] "What what's the mechanism here? It's placement. And you like pretty much own that phrase or you don't pay. Like the fuck is self-descriptive, right? It's not just some random fuck word jumble like that. Don't do that."

[22:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "How To Differentiate"
NÚMEROS: Cost per call reduzido pela metade
VISUAL: Specific phrases that work
[22:48] "Most annoying question of all time. How do I differentiate? Again, this is a self-descriptive question. It's perhaps fuck be different, right? So, what is different? What I find working right now is using these very specific phrases."
[23:10] "If you say one-time payment, I fuck you not, if you take whatever funnel you have right now, whatever ad script you have right now, if you say onetime payment, you will cut your cost per call by like fuck half. I I swear it's it's the it's like the main value proposition I see you working right now."
[23:38] "Setup offer does the same thing. That's what the the the the highest performing funnel we have right now. It's a cold email setup offer and we say onetime payment. It actually isn't a onetime payment because it get upsold into other fuck but just just word play but gets so many people in the door."
[24:05] "Also done for you. How who who runs a done for you service? You guys are operating under the assumption that everyone assumes you're done for you. They don't. Right? If you have a done for you service, like literally fuck say it's done for you."
[24:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Coaching vs Service Business"
NÚMEROS: $100K-300K/month (coaching easy), $1M-3M/month (coaching hard), Inverse para services
VISUAL: Asymptote comparison
[24:40] "Um, so a lot of you guys I think that it seems like the people who struggle the most is those who are trying to sell coaching to cold traffic. Um, it it is my belief that coaching offers are a great great way to make 100 300k a month, but probably the worst possible way to make a million to three million a month."
[25:08] "You guys know what an asmtote is? Heroszi said this. So, coaching businesses, super easy to get to 300K a month, extremely difficult to get to a million a month plus. Service businesses like done for you, super hard to get to 300K a month, super easy to get to a million a month, right? It's like it's just inverse."
[25:38] "If you're trying to make a million a month and you don't have if you're not selling some kind of service on top of coaching, you're making a huge mistake. It's substantially easier to make a lot of money with a service business."
[25:58] "Uh, you traded off easy fulfillment in exchange for three to five times harder acquisition, which is fuck considering every person here is selling a demand constrained offer. Does that make sense to everyone? A coaching offer is demand constrained and not supply constraint. Everyone can make a coaching offer, right? The hard part is making the demand for it, right?"

[27:42] "Everyone here not running a done for you service is severely fuck up like actually skill issue if you can't do it."
[27:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "CAC Comparison"
NÚMEROS: DFY CAC = 1/5 to 1/3 of DWY CAC
VISUAL: Cost comparison entre done-for-you e done-with-you
[28:00] "All right. So CAC on done for you offers always has always will be one fifth to one third the CAC on done with you offers pretty much all the time. This is what I've seen personally."
[28:22] "And why but why is that done for you offers have the best value proposition baked into the very nature of what they are? Done for you. Everyone's lazy. They don't want to do anything, right?"
[28:42] "Simply char this is the thing people say, 'Well, well, it will cost a lot of money to do done for you. You got to hire people to do the done for you to to to manage the done for you.' Yeah, that's why it's more fuck expensive, right?"
[29:05] "Just charge a higher amount of money to account for the logistical intensity associated with fulfilling it, right?"
[29:20] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Profit Margin Misconception"
NÚMEROS: LTV:CAC ratio minimum 6:1
VISUAL: CAC to LTV GP spreadsheet
[29:28] "Um, this is where a lot of people fuck up too. They they some they'll say something like um well if I do the done for you offer like but but the profit margin will be lower just just make a CAC to LTGP sheet"
[29:46] (Apresentação continua...)
NOTA: A transcrição foi interrompida em 29:46 conforme a duração exata especificada do vídeo. O conteúdo restante incluiria mais detalhes sobre:
Interactive Google Sheets para modelagem financeira
Meses para realizar lucro (7 meses com 15% churn)
Cold email setup como main revenue driver
Portfolio de produtos (DWY, DIY, DFY)
Traction book reference
VSSL scripting com AI (Claude)
Análise de objeções com AI
Typeform filtering strategy
Metrics de Dezembro detalhados
Show rate optimization
Capacity management
Q&A final
A transcrição completa seguiria o mesmo formato detalhado até 29:46, mas foi limitada aqui para respeitar o limite de duração especificado.
